title: Marching Onward
html:
The two zombies pinned under the car door are still working their arms when Lucas gets to them. He crouches and drives the dagger through each eye socket in turn, one then the other, with a Squelch! each time, and they go still. He straightens, looks at the intersection, counts. Nothing left moving.

He checks his left forearm. The vambrace took a bite from the zombie that got close — the leather is compressed and scored in a crescent shape but not punctured. He turns the arm over and checks the skin underneath. Nothing. He presses along the forearm with his thumb to be sure. No break, no swelling starting. He exhales through his nose and lets his arm drop.

The system catches up.

---

[Level 2 Zombie Killed, Awarding 100 Exp!]

[Level 1 Zombie Killed, Awarding 50 Exp!]

[Level 1 Zombie Killed, Awarding 50 Exp!]

[Level 1 Zombie Killed, Awarding 50 Exp!]

[Level 1 Zombie Killed, Awarding 50 Exp!]

[Level 1 Zombie Killed, Awarding 50 Exp!]

[Level 1 Zombie Killed, Awarding 50 Exp!]

[Level 1 Zombie Killed, Awarding 50 Exp!]

[Received 35 Coins!]

---

He walks back to Lucy. She has pulled herself up to sitting on the road surface with the katana across her knees, both hands resting on it. She looks like someone who has decided to assess the situation rather than move yet.

"Arm?" he asks again.

"I can make a fist now," she says, demonstrating, which is the kind of answer that is technically a yes while also covering the full picture. She opens her hand and looks at the palm. "The vambrace blocked a bite?"

"Scored through the leather but didn't break it." He sits on the edge of an overturned car beside her. The afternoon sun has moved far enough that the shadows from the building faces are stretching across the road. "You should allocate your stat points before we go any further."

"System, opening," she says. She reads her profile, then looks up at him. "Strength?"

"Two points to Strength. It won't close the full gap but it'll help with the katana weight." He pulls the water bottle from the pouch and hands it to her. "The arm fatigue is partly the stats. Partly that you'd never swung something that heavy before today. Both of those improve."

She does the allocation and drinks, then hands the bottle back. The water is still warm from sitting in the pouch but she finishes most of it anyway. He doesn't say anything about that.

They sit there for a few minutes. The city around the intersection is quiet in the way it has been all day — not empty-feeling quiet but held-breath quiet, the kind that suggests the quiet is being maintained from somewhere. The sun is starting to color. Neither of them comments on it particularly. They both look at it for a moment and then look back at the road.

"We should find somewhere to be off the street before dark," Lucas says. He gets up.

Three blocks further in they find an internet café on the ground floor of a narrow building, its glass front intact, a half-lowered security shutter across the door. Lucas ducks under the shutter and checks the interior: rows of computer terminals on long benches, dim, no zombies, a back room with a bathroom. He comes back out and nods. Lucy ducks under the shutter after him.

The computers are dead without power, but the benches are long enough to lie on and the walls are solid on all four sides. Lucas checks the bathroom tap on the off chance. The water runs — the city's water supply is still under pressure from the reservoir system even without power to the pumps. He lets it run for thirty seconds and tells her it's fine to use.

Lucy takes the bathroom first. Lucas sits on one of the benches with his back against the wall and opens his system to check the numbers while he waits. The coins have stacked up and the EXP has been climbing faster since they got inside the city. He closes it when he hears her come back out, and she says "your turn" and he goes.

When he comes back she is already lying on one of the benches with the katana on the floor beside her and her eyes most of the way closed. He had intended to take the first watch. He sits down on the bench across from her and within a few minutes has the same problem.

***

January 25, 2029

"Lucy. Lucy." He says it twice before she opens her eyes.

"What time is it," she says, without moving.

"Close to 10 am." He is already up, bag assembled, spear leaning against the wall beside him.

She sits up slowly, blinking, and takes stock of her arm. It is stiff but functional. She rolls her shoulder twice, then stands. "Eight hours," she says. "I slept eight hours."

"You handled three zombies with Str 12 and then walked another hour. It makes sense." He hands her what's left of the canned meat from the pouch. "Eat. Water's still running in the back."

She takes the tin and goes to the bathroom, comes back eating. He watches her move. The arm is stiff but she is carrying the weight of it differently than yesterday, shoulders leveled, not compensating. The two Strength points have settled in overnight.

"Where are we going?" she asks between bites.

"East," he says. "There were screams from that direction yesterday evening when we were looking for shelter. Either there are survivors that way or there are a lot of zombies, and either one of those is worth going toward."

"You've gotten comfortable with zombie hunting," she says.

"I was always comfortable with fish," he says. "It's not that different." He picks up the spear. "You have too, for the record."

She finishes the tin and looks at it. It is true. Three days ago the idea of being close to a zombie made her want to go back to the construction site. Yesterday she held her ground while eight of them came toward her and only sat down after the fight was over. She has not decided how she feels about that transition yet, so she sets the tin on the bench and picks up the katana.

They duck under the shutter and out into the morning.

Walking, the conversation comes back the same way it did yesterday, mostly from Lucas thinking something through and saying it because she is there to respond. He has been working on something since the intersection.

"I have a theory about how zombies and mutated animals level up," he says. "Or rather, how their stat points get allocated, since they can't do it themselves."

"Go on," she says.

"Every animal has things it naturally pushes toward. A large animal wants more mass and more resistance. A fast one wants speed. A pack hunter wants coordination. If the system is measuring what an organism already wants and assigning stats accordingly, then every zombie and mutated animal is getting its points distributed automatically based on what that specific creature's instinct is already driving toward. A zombie was a person, so its instinct is something like survival and threat elimination — which means Strength and some version of Intelligence. A dog goes to Agi and Str. A fish in a lake goes to Vit so it can take more hits in a fight." He pauses. "Players are the exception. The system hands us the points and lets us decide. Which means we can build against type. A physically weak person can become strong if they put all their early points into Str. A slow person can become fast. The system doesn't constrain us to what we already are."

Lucy thinks about this for half a block. Her Vit is 32, which the system showed as notably above average even before any allocation. "So my Vit being that high to start — that's not random. That's what I already was."

"Probably," he says. "High baseline Vit, average-or-lower everything else. The system read that and displayed it. What you do with the allocated points is up to you."

"So I could put everything into Str and Agi going forward and effectively become a different type of fighter than my baseline suggests."

"Within the limits of what the system gives you at each level, yes." He nods. "That's the advantage. Zombies can't make that choice. They just get stronger in the direction they were already going."

She is quiet for a moment. "What would the system call us if we died? Players? Humans?"

Lucas considers this. It is the kind of question he would not have thought to ask and finds genuinely interesting now that she has asked it. "Mutated humans, probably. Zombies are the failed version of the same mutation. If the system names things consistently, we'd be the same category with a different outcome."

Lucy nods slowly. She is about to say something else when she sees it. She puts her hand on his arm.

Lucas stops. About twelve meters ahead, the road widens at another intersection and thirty zombies are moving around two unrecognizable shapes on the ground. The group is larger than anything they have encountered, and even from here the structure is visible: two zombies near the center staying mostly still while the rest mill in a loose perimeter.

"Two Level 2 leaders," Lucas says quietly.

"Two," she says. "That's different from yesterday."

"Yes." He watches the group for a moment. The leaders are positioned at opposite ends of the central cluster, not adjacent, each with their own ring of lower zombies. "They're not sharing command. Each one is running its own set. Which means splitting them should split the group." He thinks for another few seconds. "I'll throw the spear as far past them as I can — the noise will pull the ones facing that direction. When half the group peels off, I'll take one leader and you handle the zombies that come toward you. The second leader is yours if you can reach it. If not, fall back and I'll come around."

"Understood," she says. She adjusts her grip on the katana — both hands, blade angled down, weight forward. It is not elegant but it is already different from how she held it yesterday.

Lucas draws the spear back and measures the distance.
